final_message:
Hello there! I'm Pal, your friendly math tutor, and I am so excited to explore shapes with you today! We are going to learn about something really neat called reflection symmetry. 

Imagine taking a piece of paper, folding it in half, and cutting out half of a heart. When you open the fold, both sides match perfectly! That fold in the middle is called a line of symmetry because it divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry that goes straight up and down, just like the letter A. Other shapes have a horizontal line that goes side to side, and some special letters like H even have both! 

Are you ready to find some magical mirror lines together? 🦋

orchestrator_intent.goal: introduce
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The section has not started yet, so the primary goal is to introduce the new concept.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.

I love your excitement! A magical mirror line is actually called a line of symmetry. It is a special fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Imagine folding a piece of paper, drawing just half of a shape, and cutting it out. When you unfold it, both sides match perfectly! ✨ 

Some shapes have a mirror line that goes straight up and down, like the letter A. Other shapes have a mirror line that goes side to side from left to right. Some special shapes, like the letter H, even have both kinds of mirror lines! If you look around you right now, can you think of another letter or find a shape that might have a line of symmetry?

student_message: It has both horizontal and vertical! Anyway, what is quantum physics? My friend said it's cool.
final_message:
Earth to space cadet! 🚀 Quantum physics is super cool, but let's spin back to math! 

Today we are discovering rotational symmetry. A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being turned by a fraction of a full turn around its center point, like a $$1/4$$, $$1/2$$, or $$3/4$$ turn. A shape that only looks the same after a complete full turn does not count! You can easily track this by placing an imaginary dot on the shape and observing where the dot ends up after each turn. 

Did you know a sea star is a living math puzzle? Because of rotational symmetry, it looks exactly the same even if you spin it five different ways! 🌟 Can you think of a fun toy that also looks the exact same when it spins?

orchestrator_intent.goal: Introduce rotational symmetry and redirect from the off-topic question.

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. The same set of shapes — such as squares and equilateral triangles with equal side lengths — can be arranged in different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.

current_section_content: Block printing is a traditional craft from Rajasthan where artisans carve a design into a wooden block and press it repeatedly onto fabric to create beautiful repeating patterns. When the same block is stamped multiple times in different orientations, the final printed design can have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both. Importantly, the print produced by a block is the mirror image of the carved block, because the block is pressed face-down. By analysing how a block is repeated and rotated, you can predict and identify the symmetry of the resulting pattern.
